_meta.scope: Tools and jaws Library app + shared workspace
_meta.purpose: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs). Each decision explains WHY a pattern exists so AI agents don't accidentally undo it.
_meta.format: Each ADR has: id, title, status, date, context, decision, rationale, consequences, enforcement, references

decisions.title: Use canonical shared/ modules for cross-app code
decisions.context: Both Setup Manager and Tools and jaws Library share model types (Tool, Jaw), services (localization, UI preferences), and UI components (StlPreviewWidget, PreferencesDialogBase, EditorHelpers). Without a canonical home, code gets duplicated, forks silently, and agents copy paste rather than reuse.
decisions.decision: All cross-app reusable code lives in shared/ under canonical paths. The canonical paths are declared in AGENTS.md and enforced by scripts/import_path_checker.py. No app may define its own version of a shared component.
rationale: Deterministic AI coding: agents know exactly where each shared component lives without guessing.
Cross-app consistency: localization, preferences, STL preview behave identically in both apps.
Single update point: fixing a shared bug fixes both apps automatically.
Import governance: canonical paths are machine-checkable; violations fail the quality gate.

positive: AI agents can reference canonical paths confidently
negative: Shared module changes affect both apps simultaneously (requires cross-app testing)
Shared module must remain backward-compatible until both apps update

decisions.enforcement: scripts/import_path_checker.py — 7 legacy path denial patterns + 2 cross-app denial patterns. Quality gate exit 1 on violation.

decisions.title: Separate databases per domain (tools.db + jaws.db + works.db)
decisions.context: Tool data, jaw data, and work/setup data have independent lifecycles and evolution vectors. Tools and jaws are master catalog data authored in the Tool Library. Works are created in Setup Manager and reference tools/jaws by ID. Each domain has its own schema, migrations, and access patterns.
decisions.decision: Three separate SQLite databases, one per domain: tool_library.db (tools), jaws_library.db (jaws), setup_manager.db (works). Each app owns its DB files. Setup Manager reads jaw/tool references by ID only; it never directly queries the Tool Library DB schema.
rationale: Domain independence: each DB can evolve schema independently without cross-app migration coordination.
Read-only boundary: Setup Manager cannot corrupt Tool Library master data; one-directional dependency by ID only.
IPC handoff preservation: apps exchange data via QLocalSocket (explicit message protocol), not shared file I/O.
Deployment flexibility: Tool Library DB can be moved, backed up, or replaced without touching Setup Manager DB.
Per README_AI.md: 'Do not add tool/jaw master schema to Setup Manager DB. Do not write into Tool Library DB files. Keep work rows ID-reference based.'

positive: Accidental cross-domain corruption impossible at the storage layer
Clear ownership: Tool Library = tool/jaw master data owner
negative: Join queries across domains require IPC or in-memory merge (no SQL JOIN across DBs)
Sync integrity managed at application level, not DB constraints

decisions.enforcement: Architectural rule; enforced by code review and module boundary checker (Phase 2+). No current automated check catches schema-adjacent coupling (identified gap in quality gate).

decisions.title: Support module extraction pattern for large UI files
decisions.context: Both home_page.py (2,223L) and work_editor_dialog.py (originally ~590L) became unmaintainable as single files. Logic for selector state, batch actions, component display, and detail panels was interleaved, making AI-assisted edits error-prone.
decisions.decision: Extract distinct responsibilities from large UI files into focused support modules in co-located _support/ directories. Each extracted module has a single responsibility. The parent file imports and orchestrates. Extraction is tracked in status docs (WORK_EDITOR_REFACTOR_STATUS.md, TOOL_EDITOR_REFACTOR.md).
rationale: Testability: focused support modules are easily unit tested; large UI classes are not.
AI-agent clarity: behavior is extracted into named modules; agents find selector logic in selector_state.py, not buried in line 1200 of home_page.py.
Safe iteration: each extraction is independently verifiable; parity tests gate each step.
Proven pattern: work_editor_dialog.py reduced 19%; tool_editor_dialog.py reduced 30% via April 2026 extraction.

positive: Monolith size reduction measurable against Phase 0 baseline
Each support module has clear single-responsibility boundary
Enables Phase 3 platform base class extraction (support modules show shared patterns)
negative: More files to navigate (15 files in home_page_support/)
Import chains grow (home_page.py imports from 15 support modules)

decisions.enforcement: Architectural guidance. Phase 3-5 will formalize by extracting remaining duplicated patterns into CatalogPageBase and EditorDialogBase (see PLATFORM_LAYER_FORWARD_SPEC.md).

decisions.title: Cross-app imports prohibited; IPC is the only inter-app communication channel
decisions.context: Setup Manager and Tools Library are separate applications that need to exchange data (when user selects a tool in Setup Manager, it should open Tool Library at that tool). Naive approach would be direct imports across app boundaries.
decisions.decision: Direct cross-app imports are categorically forbidden. Apps communicate exclusively through QLocalSocket/QLocalServer IPC (explicit message protocol). Shared code lives in shared/ only, not in one app accessed by the other.
rationale: Domain independence: each app can be deployed, versioned, or restarted independently.
Prevents circular dependencies: mutual imports between apps create unresolvable circular dependency chains.
IPC contract stability: message format version-controlled separately from implementation details.
AI determinism: agents don't need to reverse-engineer which app 'owns' a piece of code — cross-app dependencies don't exist.

positive: Apps can be developed and tested independently
IPC protocol is explicit, version-able, and introspectable
Zero cross-app import violations in current codebase
negative: Data sharing requires serialization/deserialization over IPC
Debugging requires understanding both the sender and receiver IPC message format

decisions.enforcement: scripts/import_path_checker.py — 2 cross-app denial patterns: 'from Setup Manager.' and 'from Tools and jaws Library.' Quality gate exit 1 on violation.

decisions.title: TOOLS and JAWS domains are intentionally separate within same app
decisions.context: The Tools and jaws Library app has two major domains: TOOLS (cutting tools, 70-80% of content lines) and JAWS (spindle chucks, 20-30%). These domains share similar browse/edit/export patterns but have independent data schemas, different UI concerns (jaw preview planes, spindle-side filtering), and different evolution trajectories.
decisions.decision: TOOLS and JAWS are separate module domains within the same app. They do NOT import from each other. Shared browse/edit patterns will be unified via a platform base class in Phase 3 (see PLATFORM_LAYER_FORWARD_SPEC.md). Until then, duplication is acknowledged technical debt tracked in TOOLS_JAWS_MODULAR_OVERHAUL_GOALS.md.
rationale: Different data shapes: Tool has geometry_x/z, b_axis_angle, insert codes; Jaw has clamping_diameter, spindle_side, preview_plane.
Different UI behaviors: Jaw UI has preview plane selector (XZ/XY/YZ) and spindle-side locking; Tool UI has component linking and geometry profiles.
Independent evolution: tool schema can add new geometry fields without touching jaw schema.
Phase 3 strategy: unify duplicated BEHAVIOR (catalog browsing, batch actions) without merging DATA — the platform base class pattern.

positive: No risk of tool-jaw data coupling bugs
Each domain tested independently; regressions localized
negative: 72-80% code duplication between home_page.py and jaw_page.py (acknowledged, tracked in Phase 0 baseline)
Bug fixes sometimes required in both domains (until Phase 3-5 unification)

decisions.enforcement: Per module-registry.json: home_page_support/ must not import jaw_page_support/ and vice versa. Phase 2 module_boundary_checker.py enforces intra-app domain separation.

decisions.title: Adapter pattern for intra-app service interface reuse
decisions.context: The export page logic was built for tools first. When JAW export was added, the export page expected a ToolService interface. Rather than duplicate the entire export page, an adapter bridges JawService to the ToolService interface.
decisions.decision: Use private adapter classes (_JawToolServiceAdapter, _JawExportServiceAdapter) inside jaw_export_page.py to bridge service APIs. Adapters are internal, not exported. This is intentional architecture, NOT legacy code.
rationale: Avoids duplicating 900 lines of export_page.py for jaw domain.
Phase 7 will replace these adapters with ExportSpecification pattern (domain-neutral export layer).
Adapters documented in deprecations.json as INTENTIONAL_ADAPTER with target removal phase.

positive: Jaws get full export functionality with minimal code
negative: Indirect call chain; debugging requires understanding adapter bridge
Must be removed in Phase 7 and replaced with proper ExportSpecification

decisions.enforcement: Documented. Adapters targeted for Phase 7 removal. module_boundary_checker.py will warn if new adapters appear outside jaw_export_page.py.
